steps.text: Establish data and measurement quality objectives.
Select survey methods and equipment.
Perform and confirm calibrations.
Conduct surveys.
Record results.
Generate preliminary and final conclusions.
Subtract the AL (here equal to zero) from the survey unit measurements.
Perform a one-sided WRS test on the adjusted measurements.
Perform the Quantile test on the adjusted survey unit measurements.
Judge the survey unit as not exceeding the release criteria.
Conduct retrospective power analysis to show that the data strongly support the null hypothesis.
Select a method uncertainty.
Make measurements.
Determine detection.
Evaluate the Minimum Detectable Concentration (MDC).
Categorize the types of uncertainty used into Type A (based on valid statistical methods) and Type B (based on scientific judgment).
Describe the method of determination for the categorized uncertainties in the project documents.
Include the uncertainties in the final determination of measurement uncertainty.
Apply the unity rule (Equation 8-4) to ensure that the total dose is within the release criteria.
Establish the distance, d, between the source and detector.
Establish the longest dimension of the area of elevated activity, L, according to a quantitative criterion.
Verify that the distance, d, between the DRP and detector exceeds three times the longest dimension of the area of elevated activity, L (d > 3L).
Anticipate the measurement of area/volumetric and point sources in the Measurement Quality Objectives for all remaining survey activities.
Establish separate measurement detection limits and measurement uncertainty requirements.
Initiate a targeted investigation.
Address the 'not available' Approximate Cost on Page H-56, lines 5-6.
Either add a discussion of field usage or remove the reference to it on Page H-57, line 2.
Update costs to 2020 in Table H.2, column headers, 5th column.
Clarify if the cost in Table H.2 is just for the probe or includes the instrument.
Clarify what 'low-resolution' spectrometers are being referred to in Table H.2, 2nd row.
Verify and correct the text in Table H.3, 4th row, 4th column regarding the LSC process.
Include 'Direct Ion Storage' (DIS) devices in Appendix H.
List performance specifications for each instrument in Tables H.2-H.8.
Resolve inconsistency between Table H.1 and Table H.7.
Add ANSI/AARST 2014 and 2015 to the Radon Bibliography.
Select nine potential sampling sites and measure the easy-to-measure attribute at each of these.
Divide the sites randomly into three groups of three and rank the observations within each group.

From the first group, select the site with the highest contamination level (as determined by the easy-to-measure attribute), the site with the middle level in the second group, and the site with the lowest level in the third group.
Measure the hard-to-detect radionuclide at each of the three selected sites.
Repeat the procedure as many times as needed to achieve the total number of measurements required by the Measurement Quality Objectives.
Set the Lower Bound of the Gray Region (LBGR) at a conservative (slightly higher) estimate of the mean concentration of residual radioactive material that is thought to remain in the site.
Set the LBGR to a value near to the median seen in preliminary data.
Adhere to a heuristic rule (such as using ½ of the DCGLW) to set the LBGR.
Base the LBGR on a gray region that is 1.67 times the standard deviation (σ) of the preliminary measurements.
Assume that the standard deviation (σ) of the measurement data is 30% of the DCGLW.

Evaluate whether the proposed implementation of the concept of Measurement Quality Objectives is adequately and correctly described, including the concept of measurement uncertainty (Chapter 4 and Appendix D).
Evaluate whether the proposed calculation of measurement uncertainty is consistent with the concept of Measurement Quality Objectives.
Evaluate whether the method is appropriate and practical for both laboratory and field (including scan) measurements.
Comment on the concerns of stakeholders that calculating measurement uncertainty for field measurements makes the survey process difficult to implement.

Comment on whether recommendations provided by NIST, ANSI/IEEE and MARLAP for measurement quantifiability should be incorporated further into MARSSIM, Revision 2, or whether the current recommendations should be left as is.
Add a reference to Table 5.5 earlier in Section 5.3.6.
Quantify the range of typical scanning coverage on Page 5-42, line 25 to replace the statement 'a much larger portion'.
Relabel 'Scan Area' to '% of Scan Area' in Equation 5-10 on Page 5-43.
Correct Table D.2 for Scenario B when the true condition exceeds release criterion so that the decision error from Accepting H0 is described as 'incorrectly release survey unit'.
Correct Table D.2 for Scenario B when the true condition meets release criterion so that the decision error from Rejecting H0 is described as 'incorrectly fail to release survey unit'.
Revise wording on Page D-25, line 18 to ensure associations of Type II errors with alpha for Scenario B and beta for Scenario A agree with Tables D.1 and D.2.
Correct wording on Page D-25, lines 4-5 regarding site-specific area factors.
Add the meanings of abbreviations LBGR and DCGLw as a footnote to Figure 5-7 on Page 5-28.
Add the meanings of abbreviations AL and DL as a footnote to Figure 5-8 on Page 5-29.
Either use the abbreviations yc and yD in the text or remove them from the tables.
On Page 4-9, Section 4.4, Line 15-16, change the sentence to read '.....each fraction, (f), is determined.....'.
On Page 5-36, line 3, change the text to read '...treatment of areas of elevated radioactive materials...'.
Define 'A (grid area)' and 'A (surface area)' in 'Symbols, Nomenclature, and Notations' on Page xxviii.
Remove Equations 5-5 and 5-6 on Page 5-37 to avoid potential confusion.
Clarify the source of the initial estimate of 86.6 m2 in Page 5-41, Example 8.
Clarify the Unity Rule derivation with regard to DCGLEMC and DCGLW in Equation 8-4 on Page 8-47, Section 8.6.2.
Mention Appendix O in Example 15 on Page 8-45, Section 8.6.
Add a statement to Section 5.1, Introduction, indicating that Appendix O provides detailed calculations, illustrative examples, and key statistical concepts.
Add a statement to Section 5.3.3 indicating that additional information on the WRS Test is provided in Appendix O (O.2).
Add a statement to Section 5.3.4 indicating that additional information on the Sign Test is provided in Appendix O (O.3).
Add a statement to Section 5.3.5 indicating that additional information on DCLGEMC is provided in Appendix O (O.4).
Add text to Section 5.3.3.2, p. 5-31, explaining that Table 5-2 values were calculated using Equation O-1 and increased by 20%.
Correct the reference in Section 5.3.5 (p. 5-36, line 3) from Section 4.2.5 to Section 4.5.2.
Update Section 5.3.5.2, Example 8, p. 5-41 to change L to 10.7 m and the resulting grid area to 99.1 m2.

preconditions.text: Applying the WRS test for Scenario B
The WRS test is not rejected
The Quantile test is also not rejected
Scenario B fails to reject the null
Residual radioactive material is found in an isolated area of elevated activity in addition to residual radioactive material distributed relatively uniformly across the survey unit.
A DRP is discovered and the operator seeks to identify if conditions preclude the use of the EMC process.
Historical analyses or preliminary surveys reveal the presence of DRPs (Discrete Radioactive Particles)
Change in count rate occurs over a small distance (e.g., detector reads a fraction of the maximum count rate over a small threshold distance)
Scenario A applies, where the null hypothesis is that the site contains residual radioactivity at or above the allowed DCGLW.

constraints.text: Clearly annotate relevant uncertainties (including nonstatistical/Type B uncertainties).
Retrospective power calculations are considered essential before accepting null results.
The burden of proof remains on the MARSSIM user to show that the data strongly support the null hypothesis.

Equation 6-5 only applies to detection systems that operate in pulse or counting mode; it is not applicable to current mode detection systems, such as an ion chamber or thermoluminescent dosimeter (TLD).
Only use an a posteriori determination based on critical level (or decision level) and measurement uncertainty or calculation of a confidence interval to determine detection.
Evaluate MDC with all known sources of uncertainty being properly quantified and combined into an appropriate expanded uncertainty.
Ensure the scan MDC and measurement method uncertainty are sufficient to meet MQOs to both quantify the average concentration of the radioactive material and to identify areas of elevated activity.
The use of the Unity Rule when discrete radioactive particles (DRPs) are present is not recommended.
DRPs can be extremely mobile, potentially jumping to adjacent areas, attaching to clothing, and contaminating instruments due to electrostatic charge.
Mobility and contamination risks posed by DRPs
The method is not typically applied in practice for groups of size larger than five.
